### Disclaimer — This list was generated by ChatGPT.

# Burrito Refined Native Standard Library Recommendations

The goal is a standard library that makes Burrito feel:

* practical
* ergonomic
* scriptable
* game/tool friendly
* lightweight but capable

---

# Core Philosophy

The most valuable natives are the ones users would otherwise rewrite constantly.

A good standard library should:

1. eliminate boilerplate
2. make common tasks one-liners
3. support scripting and automation naturally
4. expose practical system capabilities
5. avoid giant APIs with little day-to-day value

---

# Highest Priority Modules

If you only focus on a few areas, prioritize:

1. string utilities
2. array utilities
3. filesystem/path helpers
4. JSON
5. process execution
6. HTTP
7. regex

Those unlock most real-world scripting/tooling use cases.

---

# `bio` — Filesystem / I/O

## Recommended Additions

### Path Utilities

These are extremely high ROI.

* [ ] `joinPath(parts)`
* [ ] `baseName(path)`
* [ ] `dirName(path)`
* [ ] `extension(path)`
* [ ] `absolutePath(path)`
* [ ] `normalizePath(path)`

### File Metadata

* [ ] `fileSize(path)`
* [ ] `isFile(path)`
* [ ] `isDir(path)`
* [ ] `modifiedTime(path)`

### File Operations

* [ ] `copyFile(src, dst)`
* [ ] `moveFile(src, dst)`
* [ ] `makeDirs(path)`

### Terminal Helpers

* [ ] `printErr(x)`

---

# `bstring` — Strings

This is one of the most important modules in any practical language.

## Essential

* [x] `startsWith(s, prefix)`
* [x] `endsWith(s, suffix)`
* [x] `contains(s, sub)`
* [x] `replace(s, from, to)`
* [x] `replaceAll(s, from, to)`
* [x] `repeat(s, n)`
* [ ] `trimLeft(s)`
* [ ] `trimRight(s)`
* [ ] `lastIndexOf(s, sub)`

## Parsing / Conversion

* [ ] `parseInt(s)`
* [ ] `parseFloat(s)`
* [ ] `parseBool(s)`

## Formatting

* [ ] `padLeft(s, len, ch)`
* [ ] `padRight(s, len, ch)`
* [ ] `capitalize(s)`

## Validation

* [x] `isDigit(s)`
* [x] `isAlpha(s)`
* [x] `isAlphaNumeric(s)`

---

# `barray` — Arrays

Arrays are heavily used everywhere, so convenience matters.

## Essential

* [ ] `shift(arr)`
* [ ] `unshift(arr, val)`
* [ ] `findIndex(arr, fn)`
* [ ] `lastIndexOf(arr, val)`

## Functional

* [x] `every(arr, fn)`
* [x] `some(arr, fn)`
* [x] `flatten(arr)`
* [ ] `flatMap(arr, fn)`

## Collection Helpers

* [ ] `unique(arr)`
* [ ] `shuffle(arr)`
* [ ] `first(arr)`
* [ ] `last(arr)`
* [ ] `isEmpty(arr)`

## Non-Mutating Variants

* [x] `sorted(arr, fn)`

---

# `bmath` — Math

The current coverage is already good.

## High-Value Additions

* [ ] `clamp(x, lo, hi)`
* [ ] `sign(x)`
* [ ] `lerp(a, b, t)`
* [ ] `remap(x, a1, a2, b1, b2)`

## Statistics

* [ ] `sum(arr)`
* [ ] `mean(arr)`
* [ ] `median(arr)`

## Geometry / Games

* [x] `degToRad(x)`
* [x] `radToDeg(x)`
* [x] `distance(x1, y1, x2, y2)`
* [x] `hypot(x, y)`

---

# `bmap` — Maps / Dictionaries

Burrito already has map infrastructure internally.

This should become a first-class practical module.

## Recommended Core API

* [ ] `mapValues(m)`
* [ ] `mapEntries(m)`
* [ ] `mapSize(m)`
* [ ] `mapCopy(m)`
* [ ] `mapMerge(a, b)`

## Not Recommended

A dedicated `bset` module is unnecessary initially.

Maps already cover most set-like use cases.

---

# `bcast` — Casting / Reflection

Keep this small and practical.

## Core

* [ ] `toNumber(x)`
* [ ] `toBool(x)`
* [ ] `toString(x)`

## Useful Reflection

* [ ] `isNumber(x)`
* [ ] `isString(x)`
* [ ] `isArray(x)`
* [ ] `isMap(x)`
* [ ] `isFunction(x)`
* [ ] `isNull(x)`
* [ ] etc.

## Intentionally Avoided

Do not build a giant reflection API yet.

The current runtime architecture already exposes enough dynamic behavior.

---

# `btime` — Time

Keep this focused and lightweight.

## Core

* [ ] `timeMs()`
* [ ] `formatTime(timestamp, fmt)`

## Optional

* [ ] `parseTime(s, fmt)`

## Intentionally Removed

Avoid large calendar/date decomposition APIs initially.

These add a surprising amount of implementation complexity.

---

# `bsys` — System

This module has enormous practical value.

## Environment

* [ ] `args()`
* [ ] `env(name)`
* [ ] `setEnv(name, value)`
* [ ] `homeDir()`
* [ ] `tempDir()`

## Execution

* [ ] `run(cmd)`

## Platform

* [ ] `platform()`
* [ ] `arch()`

## Process

* [ ] `pid()`
* [ ] `exit(code)`

## Terminal

* [ ] `clearScreen()`
* [ ] `terminalWidth()`
* [ ] `terminalHeight()`

---

# `bjson` — JSON

This is one of the most important modules for practical programming.

## Essential

* [ ] `jsonParse(s)`
* [ ] `jsonStringify(x)`

JSON + maps immediately unlock:

* APIs
* config files
* automation
* tooling
* backend utilities

---

# `bregex` — Regex

Regex support massively increases scripting capability.

## Recommended Minimal API

* [ ] `regexMatch(pattern, s)`
* [ ] `regexFind(pattern, s)`
* [ ] `regexReplace(pattern, s, repl)`
* [ ] `regexSplit(pattern, s)`

## Recommendation

Keep the surface small initially.

You do not need a giant PCRE-style API to get enormous practical value.

---

# `bnet` — Networking

This should focus on practical HTTP first.

## High-Value HTTP

* [ ] `httpGet(url)`
* [ ] `httpPost(url, body)`
* [ ] `httpRequest(method, url, headers, body)`

## JSON Helpers

* [ ] `httpGetJson(url)`

## Optional

* [ ] `downloadFile(url, path)`

## Intentionally Deferred

Raw sockets can wait until later:

* `socketOpen`
* `socketSend`
* `socketReceive`
* `socketClose`

HTTP gives vastly more day-to-day value.

---

# `bgraphics` — Graphics

If Burrito continues targeting games/tools, these are valuable.

## Input

* `getMousePos()`
* `isMouseDown(button)`
* `isKeyDown(key)`
* `isKeyPressed(key)`

## Collision Helpers

* [ ] `rectOverlap(...)`
* [ ] `pointInRect(...)`

---

# `bcrypto` — Small but High ROI

Very useful for real applications.

## Hashing

* [ ] `sha256(s)`
* [ ] `md5(s)`

## Encoding

* [ ] `base64Encode(s)`
* [ ] `base64Decode(s)`
* [ ] `hexEncode(bytes)`
* [ ] `hexDecode(s)`

## Random

* [ ] `secureRandomBytes(n)`

---

# `blog` — Logging

Tiny module, huge usability win.

## Recommended

* [ ] `info(msg)`
* [ ] `warn(msg)`
* [ ] `error(msg)`
* [ ] `debug(msg)`

Optional:

* colors
* timestamps
* log levels

---

# Recommended Development Order

## Phase 1 — Make Burrito Feel Practical

Implement first:

* string completion
* array completion
* path utilities
* maps
* JSON
* args/env/cwd/run

This is the biggest “language maturity” jump.

---

## Phase 2 — Make Burrito Useful for Tooling

Then:

* regex
* filesystem metadata
* HTTP
* logging
* formatting helpers

At this point Burrito becomes genuinely useful for:

* CLIs
* automation
* scripting
* API tooling
* config manipulation

---

## Phase 3 — Make Burrito Useful for Applications

Finally:

* graphics expansion
* audio
* crypto
* timers
* async/concurrency

---

# Final Recommendation

The strongest standard library direction for Burrito is probably:

> “small, practical, batteries-included scripting language with game/tool capabilities”

—not a giant enterprise/runtime-heavy platform.

The biggest wins are therefore:

1. JSON
2. maps
3. path utilities
4. HTTP
5. regex
6. process execution
7. strong string helpers
8. strong array helpers

That combination alone covers the majority of everyday programming tasks users actually encounter.
